talk_title: Precision Approaches to HPV-Independent Head and Neck Cancer

speaker.name: Barbara Bertness
speaker.affiliation: Yale School of Medicine, Medical Oncology

tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: Pembrolizumab plus chemo HR 0.63 in PD‑L1‑rich HPV‑negative HNSCC

biomarkers.name: Tumor Mutation Burden
biomarkers.finding: TMB ≥15 associated with higher response to pembrolizumab in HPV‑negative HNSCC

biomarkers.name: p53 mutation (disruptive)
biomarkers.finding: Disruptive p53 mutations correlate with higher TMB

biomarkers.name: CDKN2A alteration
biomarkers.finding: Co‑mutation with p53 further elevates TMB

models.name: SCC61
models.genotype_or_key_features: CDC25 mutation, resistant to WEE1 inhibition

MOA_summary: Bertness highlighted that HPV‑negative HNSCC harbors EGFR overexpression and frequent loss of tumor suppressors (TP53, CDKN2A) leading to high TMB. She demonstrated that EGFR‑targeted therapies are limited by downstream resistance (PIK3CA, RAS, PTEN loss) and nuclear EGFR. Bispecific agents (EGFR/TGF‑β, EGFR/LGR5, EGFR/MET) improve response when combined with pembrolizumab. She also presented a synthetic‑lethal strategy where p53 loss creates dependence on Aurora A; combined Aurora A and WEE1 inhibition induces replication stress, mitotic catastrophe, and tumor regression in p53‑mutant models, including EGFR‑TKI‑resistant cells.
key_findings: Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy improves HR to 0.63 in PD‑L1‑rich HPV‑negative HNSCC (KEYNOTE‑048).
Ficaserafusp + pembrolizumab yields 54% response and median OS >20 months in HPV‑negative PD‑L1+ disease, now in phase III FORTIFY‑HN01.
Pitaseptumab + pembrolizumab achieves 66% ORR in HPV‑negative HNSCC, supporting two ongoing phase III trials.
Amivantamab monotherapy shows 45% response in refractory HPV‑negative HNSCC; combination with pembrolizumab reaches 56% response in first line.
Aurora A inhibition (VIC‑1911) blocks replication fork progression; combined with WEE1 inhibitor (davisertib) induces mitotic catastrophe and tumor regression in p53‑mutant HNSCC xenografts and PDXs.
Synergy of Aurora A + WEE1 inhibition is lost in CDC25‑mutant SCC61, indicating genotype‑specific resistance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Nuclear EGFR evades cetuximab; adding erlotinib restores intracellular EGFR inhibition.
Loss of PTEN, PIK3CA or RAS mutations confer resistance to EGFR‑targeted therapy.
CDC25 mutation confers resistance to WEE1 inhibition, limiting Aurora‑WEE1 synergy in SCC61.

combination_strategies.rationale: PD‑1 blockade synergizes with EGFR bispecifics by enhancing anti‑tumor immunity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Response rate 54% (ficaserafusp) and 66% (pitaseptumab) in HPV‑negative PD‑L1+ HNSCC

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care backbone for KEYNOTE‑048
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved HR to 0.63 in PD‑L1‑rich cohort

combination_strategies.rationale: Aurora inhibition activates WEE1‑mediated arrest; dual inhibition forces mitotic entry with defective spindles
combination_strategies.phenotype: Mitotic catastrophe and tumor regression in p53‑mutant models

combination_strategies.rationale: Eradicates nuclear EGFR that cetuximab cannot bind
combination_strategies.phenotype: ORR increased from 33% to 62.5% when added after cetuximab+chemo

open_questions: Which specific p53 disruptive mutations predict maximal synergy with Aurora‑WEE1 inhibition?
Can biomarkers such as CDC25 status be used to pre‑exclude non‑responders to the Aurora‑WEE1 combo?

talk_title: HPV‑Associated Head and Neck Cancer Subtypes and Radiation Sensitivity

speaker.name: Dale Yarbrough
speaker.affiliation: University of North Carolina, Chair of Otolaryngology

biomarkers.name: NF‑kappaB activation signature
biomarkers.finding: Defines low‑risk (high NF‑kappaB) vs high‑risk subtypes with HR >3.5 for survival

biomarkers.name: TRAF3/CYLD mutation status
biomarkers.finding: Mutations associate with improved survival and low‑risk subtype

biomarkers.name: Circulating tumor DNA (HPV ctDNA)
biomarkers.finding: Higher baseline ctDNA in high‑risk subtype; potential stratifier for de‑escalation

clinical_data.metric: Hazard Ratio for survival (high vs low risk)
clinical_data.population: HPV+ HNSCC patients across multiple cohorts

clinical_data.value: significant increase in cell kill after TRAF3 knockout
cure in 1 mouse, improved survival in cohort

MOA_summary: Yarbrough defined two HPV‑positive HNSCC subtypes based on NF‑kappaB activation driven by TRAF3 or CYLD loss; low‑risk tumors exhibit high NF‑kappaB activity and intrinsic radiation sensitivity, while high‑risk tumors are radio‑resistant. Activation of NF‑kappaB via TLR5 agonist entolimod sensitizes high‑risk tumors to radiation, offering a therapeutic avenue to improve the therapeutic ratio.
key_findings: HPV+ HNSCC segregates into low‑risk (high NF‑kappaB, TRAF3/CYLD loss) and high‑risk subtypes with hazard ratios >3.5 for overall survival.
Low‑risk subtype shows markedly increased radiation sensitivity in vitro and in vivo.
NF‑kappaB activation via TLR5 agonist entolimod synergizes with radiation to cure high‑risk mouse tumors while protecting normal tissue.
Birinapant (SMAC mimetic) plus chemoradiation worsened survival, leading to abandonment of that strategy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High‑risk tumors resist radiation due to lack of NF‑kappaB‑driven pro‑apoptotic signaling.
SMAC mimetic trial demonstrated unexpected detrimental effect, highlighting context‑dependent NF‑kappaB modulation.

combination_strategies.rationale: NF‑kappaB activation may lower the threshold for radiation‑induced cell death
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor control and cure in high‑risk mouse models
Worse overall survival in phase II trial (TrialNEX)

open_questions: Can entolimod be safely combined with definitive radiation in humans and improve outcomes for high‑risk HPV+ HNSCC?
What additional biomarkers (e.g., integration status, episomal HPV) refine risk beyond NF‑kappaB signature?

talk_title: Minimal Residual Disease and Treatment Optimization in Head and Neck Cancer

speaker.name: Ari Rosenberg
speaker.affiliation: University of Chicago, Associate Professor of Medical Oncology

tumor_types: HPV-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: HPV ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: Baseline detection 86%; clearance by mid‑treatment predicts superior PFS

biomarkers.name: Tumor‑informed ctDNA panel
biomarkers.finding: Post‑operative ctDNA positivity predicts recurrence with 100% PPV in small cohorts

biomarkers.name: Methylation‑based ctDNA assay
biomarkers.finding: Detects MRD in HPV‑negative HNSCC with high specificity when multiple time points used

models.name: DART de‑escalation study
models.genotype_or_key_features: Post‑op HPV+ HNSCC, ctDNA‑stratified radiation dose

clinical_data.population: HPV+ HNSCC, low clinical risk or favorable ctDNA kinetics
Post‑op HPV+ HNSCC, ctDNA‑negative received 36 Gy

MOA_summary: Rosenberg presented ctDNA as a minimally invasive biomarker for MRD across HPV‑positive and HPV‑negative HNSCC. Early clearance of HPV ctDNA during neoadjuvant therapy predicts favorable PFS, while post‑treatment ctDNA positivity forecasts recurrence. Adaptive trial designs using ctDNA dynamics to de‑escalate radiation or chemotherapy, or to intensify immunotherapy, demonstrate improved response rates and survival, illustrating how real‑time molecular monitoring can guide treatment optimization.
key_findings: Baseline HPV ctDNA detected in 86% of patients; mid‑treatment clearance (>95%) identifies a cohort with superior PFS.
In the OPTIMA 2 neoadjuvant trial, ctDNA clearance after neoadjuvant chemo‑immunotherapy correlated with improved PFS.
SYNERGY phase II trial using ctDNA‑guided adaptive chemo‑immunotherapy achieved 63% ORR, median OS 30.9 months, and allowed de‑escalation of chemotherapy in ~75% of patients.
Post‑operative ctDNA positivity predicts recurrence with 100% specificity in early studies, supporting its use for risk‑adapted radiation de‑escalation (DART trial).
Methylation‑based ctDNA assays provide MRD detection in HPV‑negative HNSCC, especially when serial sampling is employed.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: A subset of patients remained ctDNA‑negative despite eventual recurrence, indicating limits of assay sensitivity.
SMAC mimetic trial failure underscores that not all NF‑kappaB activation strategies translate to benefit.

combination_strategies.rationale: Patients with low‑risk ctDNA profile may tolerate radiation de‑escalation without compromising control
combination_strategies.phenotype: 90% 2‑year PFS in low‑risk or favorable ctDNA groups

combination_strategies.rationale: Target MRD‑positive patients post‑definitive therapy to prevent recurrence
combination_strategies.phenotype: Trial ongoing; early data suggest clearance in treated patient

combination_strategies.rationale: ctDNA rise after initial PD‑1 monotherapy triggers addition of chemotherapy in SYNERGY trial
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved ORR and survival compared to static treatment

open_questions: What is the optimal ctDNA assay (tumor‑informed vs tumor‑agnostic) for early detection of MRD in HPV‑negative HNSCC?
Can ctDNA dynamics be integrated with imaging and other biomarkers to refine adaptive treatment algorithms?
